threats.title: closeMarket Underflow: availableLiquidity -= normalizedAmountPaid Uses Raw Subtraction Instead of satSub
threats.hypothesis_description: In closeMarket(), the loop processing unpaid withdrawal batches uses `availableLiquidity -= normalizedAmountPaid` (raw subtraction), whereas the equivalent code in repayAndProcessUnpaidWithdrawalBatches() uses `availableLiquidity.satSub(normalizedAmountPaid)`. Inside _applyWithdrawalBatchPayment, `scaleAmount` (rayDiv, rounds to nearest) can produce a scaledAmountBurned that, when converted back via mulDiv (rounds down differently), yields a normalizedAmountPaid exceeding the original availableLiquidity input. With scaleFactor >= 2*RAY and small remaining liquidity, scaleAmount(1) can return 1, but mulDiv(1, scaleFactor, RAY) returns 2, causing arithmetic underflow.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a direct code inconsistency between two functions performing identical logic. repayAndProcessUnpaidWithdrawalBatches explicitly guards against this with satSub, proving the developers knew the rounding issue exists. closeMarket lacks this safeguard, making it vulnerable to permanent DoS.

proof_of_signal.pattern: availableLiquidity -= normalizedAmountPaid (raw subtraction); compare with WildcatMarketWithdrawals.sol:188 which uses availableLiquidity = availableLiquidity.satSub(normalizedAmountPaid)

preconditions: Market has at least one unpaid withdrawal batch
scaleFactor has grown sufficiently (accumulated interest) so rounding in scaleAmount/mulDiv diverges
Borrower calls closeMarket() to settle all debts

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: normalizedAmountPaid from _applyWithdrawalBatchPayment can exceed availableLiquidity due to scaleAmount rounding to nearest (rayDiv) vs mulDiv rounding down, creating asymmetric conversion
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No satSub applied, unlike identical pattern in repayAndProcessUnpaidWithdrawalBatches

analysis_questions: With what scaleFactor values does the rounding divergence cause normalizedAmountPaid > availableLiquidity?
Can this occur with single batch processing or only with multi-batch compounding rounding errors?
Does the pending batch processing before the loop contribute to reducing availableLiquidity to a vulnerable level?
validation_requirements: Construct a market where closeMarket reverts due to arithmetic underflow
Verify that the same market can be resolved via repayAndProcessUnpaidWithdrawalBatches (which uses satSub)
Quantify the scaleFactor threshold and batch configurations that trigger the bug

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A borrower with an active market containing unpaid withdrawal batches attempts to close the market after accruing significant interest (scaleFactor >> RAY). The borrower deposits exact totalDebts amount, but closeMarket reverts due to arithmetic underflow in the batch processing loop. The borrower is permanently unable to close the market, continuing to accrue interest obligations indefinitely.
prerequisites: Market has accumulated interest (scaleFactor > RAY)
At least one unpaid withdrawal batch exists

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Borrower permanently unable to close market, forced to pay interest indefinitely. Lenders may be unable to withdraw if borrower stops repaying due to inability to close.
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Borrower directly; all lenders indirectly if market becomes inoperative
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Workaround may exist via repayAndProcessUnpaidWithdrawalBatches (uses satSub), but closeMarket itself is broken

threats.test_coverage_gap: closeMarket with various scaleFactor values and unpaid batch configurations

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by any borrower attempting to close a mature market

threats.title: closeMarket Rounding Dust in scaledPendingWithdrawals Prevents Market Closure
threats.hypothesis_description: In closeMarket(), after processing all pending and unpaid withdrawal batches, the function asserts `state.scaledPendingWithdrawals != 0` and reverts with CloseMarketWithUnpaidWithdrawals. Due to the rounding difference between scaleAmount (rayDiv, rounds to nearest) and the actual liquidity available, scaleAmount(availableLiquidity) may be strictly less than the remaining scaledAmountOwed by 1 wei of scaled tokens. This leaves non-zero dust in scaledPendingWithdrawals, causing the final check to fail even when the borrower has deposited sufficient assets to cover all debts.
threats.investigation_rationale: The conversion roundtrip normalizeAmount→scaleAmount is not identity-preserving. When totalDebts exactly matches currentlyHeld, the availableLiquidity equals normalizeAmount(scaledTotalSupply), but scaleAmount of this value may return slightly less than scaledTotalSupply, leaving 1 wei of scaled tokens unburned.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (state.scaledPendingWithdrawals != 0) revert_CloseMarketWithUnpaidWithdrawals() — strict zero check after rounding-lossy batch processing

preconditions: Market has pending or unpaid withdrawal batches
scaleFactor is such that scaleAmount(normalizeAmount(scaledAmountOwed)) < scaledAmountOwed
Borrower deposits exact totalDebts to close market

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Strict zero check after lossy arithmetic roundtrip
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: scaleAmount(availableLiquidity) can be less than scaledAmountOwed even when availableLiquidity = normalizeAmount(scaledAmountOwed)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No tolerance for dust in the scaledPendingWithdrawals check

analysis_questions: For which scaleFactor values does scaleAmount(normalizeAmount(x)) != x?
Can borrower deposit slightly more than totalDebts to compensate for rounding dust?
Would the excess be returned or trapped?
validation_requirements: Mathematically prove existence of scaleFactor values where the roundtrip loses precision
Construct a market deployment that cannot be closed
Verify whether depositing additional dust resolves the issue or creates other problems

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Natural occurrence for any market where interest has accrued and withdrawal batches exist. Borrower deposits exact totalDebts, but rounding leaves 1 wei in scaledPendingWithdrawals. The strict != 0 check reverts. Borrower must find workaround or remains stuck with an open market accruing 0% interest (since other close steps already executed in the memory state, but the transaction reverts).
prerequisites: Withdrawal batches exist at time of market closure
Scale factor such that roundtrip loses precision

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent inability to close market; borrower continues accruing interest obligations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Borrower primarily, all market participants indirectly
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Unknown - may require depositing excess assets above totalDebts

threats.test_coverage_gap: closeMarket with rounding edge cases across various scaleFactor values

threats.title: closeMarket Loop Repeatedly Processes Same Unpaid Batch Without Progress
threats.hypothesis_description: In closeMarket(), the loop `for (uint256 i; i < numBatches; i++)` processes unpaid batches by calling _processUnpaidWithdrawalBatch, which always reads `first()` from the unpaidBatches queue. If the first batch cannot be fully paid (due to insufficient liquidity after rounding), it remains at the front of the queue. The loop continues iterating numBatches times, re-processing the same batch with progressively less (or zero) liquidity. This wastes gas proportional to numBatches without making forward progress.
threats.investigation_rationale: Compare with repayAndProcessUnpaidWithdrawalBatches which has `while (i++ < numBatches && availableLiquidity > 0)` - the extra `availableLiquidity > 0` guard prevents useless iterations. closeMarket lacks this optimization.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for loop with numBatches iterations always calling first() without checking if progress is made; compare with WildcatMarketWithdrawals.sol:184 which checks availableLiquidity > 0

preconditions: Multiple unpaid withdrawal batches exist
First batch in queue cannot be fully paid due to liquidity constraints or rounding
closeMarket is called by borrower

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unbounded loop processing same element without progress check
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: _processUnpaidWithdrawalBatch always reads first() - if batch not fully paid, it stays at front, loop processes it again with zero additional payment
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No `availableLiquidity > 0` check in loop condition, no break on zero-progress iteration

analysis_questions: What is the gas cost per iteration when processing a batch with zero available liquidity?
Can a malicious lender create enough unpaid batches to make closeMarket exceed block gas limit?
Does the loop always terminate or can it cause OOG?
validation_requirements: Create market with many unpaid batches and attempt closeMarket
Measure gas usage per loop iteration
Determine maximum numBatches before OOG

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A malicious lender queues many small withdrawals at intervals matching the withdrawal batch duration, creating numerous unpaid batches (especially if withdrawal batch duration is very small). When the borrower attempts to close the market, the loop iterates over all batches but gets stuck processing the first one repeatedly, potentially exceeding block gas limit.
prerequisites: Many unpaid withdrawal batches exist
First batch has insufficient liquidity

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Borrower unable to close market due to gas exhaustion; continues paying interest
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Borrower could use repayAndProcessUnpaidWithdrawalBatches to clear batches first

threats.test_coverage_gap: closeMarket with large numbers of unpaid batches

threats.title: onBorrow Hook Uses Wrong Size Constant (RepayHook_Base_Size Instead of BorrowHook_Base_Size)
threats.hypothesis_description: In HooksConfig.sol's `onBorrow` function, the assembly block computes the calldata size for the hook call using `RepayHook_Base_Size` (line: `let size := add(RepayHook_Base_Size, extraCalldataBytes)`) instead of the expected `BorrowHook_Base_Size`. Both constants currently equal 0x0224, so this bug is latent. However, if borrow hook parameters are ever extended or modified independently of repay parameters, the wrong calldata size would be used, causing the hook call to receive truncated or excess data.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a demonstrable copy-paste error in the hook dispatch code. Even though both constants happen to match (0x0224), the code is semantically incorrect and should reference BorrowHook_Base_Size. Any future change to either constant would silently break borrow hook calls.

proof_of_signal.pattern: let size := add(RepayHook_Base_Size, extraCalldataBytes) — should be BorrowHook_Base_Size

preconditions: Market has useOnBorrow hook enabled
Currently both constants are 0x0224 so no immediate impact

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Wrong constant name in assembly size calculation
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Uses RepayHook_Base_Size (0x0224) where BorrowHook_Base_Size (0x0224) should be used
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No compile-time or runtime check that the correct constant is used

analysis_questions: Are BorrowHook_Base_Size and RepayHook_Base_Size always guaranteed to be equal?
Could a future code change modify one without the other?
Does this affect hook calldata decoding on the hook contract side?
validation_requirements: Verify both constants equal 0x0224
Confirm this is a copy-paste error vs intentional reuse
Assess if hook contracts rely on exact calldata size

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Currently not exploitable as constants match. If BorrowHook parameters are extended in a future version, onBorrow would send incorrectly sized calldata to the hook, potentially causing silent data corruption or revert in the hook contract, bricking the borrow function.
prerequisites: onBorrow hook enabled
Constants diverge in future code change

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Latent bug - currently zero impact; future risk of broken borrow functionality
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All markets with borrow hooks if constants diverge

threats.title: pushProtocolFeeBipsUpdates Reverts Entirely If Any Market in Range Is Closed or Has Unchanged Fees
threats.hypothesis_description: HooksFactory.pushProtocolFeeBipsUpdates iterates over markets and calls `market.setProtocolFeeBips(protocolFeeBips)` via raw assembly call. If any market in the range is closed, `setProtocolFeeBips` reverts with `ProtocolFeeChangeOnClosedMarket`. Similarly, if any market already has the target protocolFeeBips, it reverts with `ProtocolFeeNotChanged`. The assembly check `if iszero(call(...)) { revert }` causes the entire batch update to fail, blocking fee updates for all other markets using that hooks template.
threats.investigation_rationale: A single closed market or market already at the target fee rate blocks the entire batch update. The arch-controller owner must manually paginate around problematic markets. A borrower could intentionally close their market to grief the protocol fee update mechanism.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Assembly call to market.setProtocolFeeBips reverts entire transaction if any single call fails; no error handling or skip logic

preconditions: At least one market using the hooks template is closed or already has the target fee
Admin calls pushProtocolFeeBipsUpdates with a range including that market

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Batch operation reverts entirely on single failure
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: iszero(call(...)) triggers SetProtocolFeeBipsFailed revert for any failed sub-call

analysis_questions: Can the admin work around this by manually selecting market index ranges that exclude closed markets?
How many markets can be blocked by a single closed market?
Does setProtocolFeeBips also revert if hooks.onSetProtocolFeeBips fails?
validation_requirements: Deploy multiple markets under same template, close one, attempt pushProtocolFeeBipsUpdates
Verify that the admin must manually skip the closed market's index

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A borrower closes their market. The arch-controller owner attempts to update protocol fees across all markets using that template. The transaction reverts because the closed market's setProtocolFeeBips reverts. The admin must identify and exclude the closed market's index, requiring off-chain coordination for each batch update.
prerequisites: At least one closed market exists in the template's market array

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Protocol fee revenue delayed; admin operational overhead
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocol fee recipient, all markets using the same template
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Admin can work around by paginating with skip ranges

threats.test_coverage_gap: pushProtocolFeeBipsUpdates with closed markets in range

threats.title: withdrawableProtocolFees Arithmetic Underflow When Market Is Underfunded
threats.hypothesis_description: In MarketState.sol, `withdrawableProtocolFees` computes `uint256 totalAvailableAssets = totalAssets - state.normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals`. When `totalAssets < normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals` (which happens when the market is severely delinquent or the borrower has not repaid), this arithmetic underflows, causing a panic revert. The `collectFees` function calls this and will revert with an unrecoverable panic instead of a meaningful error.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function lacks a satSub or explicit check for the underflow condition. While protocol fees should not be withdrawable when the market can't cover unclaimed withdrawals, the current code panics instead of returning 0 gracefully.

proof_of_signal.pattern: totalAssets - state.normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals without underflow protection when market is delinquent

preconditions: Market holds fewer assets than normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals
Someone calls collectFees or the view function withdrawableProtocolFees

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unchecked subtraction of potentially larger value
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: totalAssets - normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals panics when market is underfunded

analysis_questions: Under what realistic conditions does totalAssets fall below normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals?
Does this block any other functionality that calls withdrawableProtocolFees internally?
Is the view function withdrawableProtocolFees() called by _getUpdatedState or _writeState?
validation_requirements: Create a delinquent market where totalAssets < normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals
Call collectFees and verify it panics
Confirm this doesn't block other critical operations

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A market becomes severely delinquent after the borrower fails to maintain reserves. The fee recipient attempts to collect accrued protocol fees but `collectFees` panics due to arithmetic underflow. The fee collection is blocked until the borrower repays enough to cover unclaimed withdrawals.
prerequisites: Market is delinquent with totalAssets < normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Fee collection temporarily blocked; no fund loss

threats.title: rescueTokens Lacks nonReentrant Modifier Allowing Potential Reentrancy via Malicious Token
threats.hypothesis_description: The `rescueTokens` function in WildcatMarket.sol has `onlyBorrower` modifier but lacks `nonReentrant`. When calling `token.safeTransferAll(msg.sender)`, if the token implements a transfer callback (e.g., ERC777 tokensReceived), the borrower could reenter other non-view functions on the market. While most state-modifying functions have `nonReentrant`, the reentrancy guard uses transient storage and is cleared between calls, so a reentrant call from rescueTokens to another nonReentrant function WOULD be blocked. However, rescueTokens itself could be reentered, potentially causing double-transfer of the same token.
threats.investigation_rationale: The absence of nonReentrant on a function with an external call to an arbitrary address (token parameter) is a code quality issue. While the onlyBorrower gate limits the attack surface, and other functions' nonReentrant guards provide partial protection, rescueTokens self-reentrancy is theoretically possible with ERC777-like tokens.

proof_of_signal.pattern: External call to arbitrary token address (token.safeTransferAll) without nonReentrant modifier

preconditions: Borrower sends an ERC777-like token to the market by accident
That token has a transfer callback mechanism
Borrower calls rescueTokens for that token
Token callback reenters rescueTokens

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: External call without reentrancy guard on function with arbitrary address parameter
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: safeTransferAll triggers external call to potentially malicious token

analysis_questions: Can rescueTokens reenter itself when the token has a callback?
On the second entry, would safeTransferAll transfer 0 (balance already drained)?
Is there any state that could be corrupted via reentrancy?
validation_requirements: Create a token with transfer callback that reenters rescueTokens
Verify that second entry transfers 0 balance (harmless)
Check if reentrancy can reach other unprotected functions

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Borrower accidentally sends ERC777 tokens to the market. When calling rescueTokens, the token callback reenters rescueTokens. The second call attempts safeTransferAll but balance is already 0 from the first transfer, so the second call is effectively a no-op. Impact is limited to gas waste.
prerequisites: ERC777-like token accidentally sent to market
Borrower calls rescueTokens

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Minimal - second call transfers 0; no state corruption

threats.title: repay() Transfers Assets Before State Update, Allowing Interest Calculation on Newly Added Assets
threats.hypothesis_description: In WildcatMarket.repay(), the asset transfer via `safeTransferFrom` occurs BEFORE `_getUpdatedState()` is called. This means when `_getUpdatedState` calculates `totalAssets()` for pending batch payments, it includes the just-transferred repayment amount. In contrast, `_repay()` (used by repayOutstandingDebt/repayDelinquentDebt) is called AFTER `_getUpdatedState()`. The repay() ordering could allow the repayment to be counted as available liquidity for pending batch payments during the state update, reducing delinquency metrics within the same interest accrual window.
threats.investigation_rationale: The ordering difference between repay() and repayOutstandingDebt()/_repay() suggests either intentional design (as in repayAndProcessUnpaidWithdrawalBatches) or an inconsistency. If intentional, the repay function allows 'early' credit of repayment toward batch payments, which could reduce delinquency time tracking in the same block.

proof_of_signal.pattern: asset.safeTransferFrom BEFORE _getUpdatedState() in repay(); compare lines 155-160 where _getUpdatedState() is called BEFORE _repay()

preconditions: Market is delinquent
repay() is called instead of repayOutstandingDebt()
Pending batch exists that can absorb the payment

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Transfer before state calculation changes totalAssets() used in interest/batch computation
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Repaid amount counted in totalAssets during _getUpdatedState interest accrual, potentially reducing delinquency detection
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No documentation explaining the intentional ordering difference

analysis_questions: Does the transfer-before-update pattern materially change interest accrual or delinquency tracking?
Can a borrower use repay() instead of repayOutstandingDebt() to avoid delinquency penalties?
Is this the same intentional pattern as repayAndProcessUnpaidWithdrawalBatches?
validation_requirements: Compare delinquency tracking outcomes between repay() and repayOutstandingDebt() for identical amounts
Check if the ordering affects penalty APR activation timing

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A borrower calls repay() strategically just before the delinquency grace period expires. Because the transfer happens before state update, the market appears well-funded during interest accrual, potentially avoiding penalty APR activation for that period.
prerequisites: Market is delinquent
Borrower uses repay() vs repayOutstandingDebt()

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Borrower avoids penalty APR, lenders receive less interest than entitled
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Design issue - would require code change

threats.test_coverage_gap: Comparing repay() vs repayOutstandingDebt() delinquency effects

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Single transaction timing relative to grace period
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Difficult to detect - requires tracking call patterns

threats.title: Deposit Amount Credited Without Verifying Actual Received Amount (Fee-on-Transfer Token Inflation)
threats.hypothesis_description: In _depositUpTo(), the scaled amount credited to the depositor is computed from the user-supplied `amount` parameter BEFORE the `safeTransferFrom` call. The function does not verify how many tokens were actually received by the market. If the underlying asset implements fee-on-transfer mechanics, the market would receive fewer tokens than `amount` but credit the full scaled amount to the depositor, inflating internal accounting. Over time, this leads to insolvency where total claims exceed actual assets.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code computes `scaledAmount = state.scaleAmount(amount)` and credits it before checking actual receipt. The project documentation states 'No fee-on-transfer tokens' but this is a deployment/configuration assumption, not enforced in code. If a previously normal token adds fees via upgrade, all markets using it become vulnerable.
deployment_assumptions: Underlying asset does not have fee-on-transfer mechanics
threats.config_evidence: Project documentation states 'No fee-on-transfer, no rebasing tokens as underlying'. No on-chain enforcement in WildcatMarket or HooksFactory. Asset blacklist in ArchController exists but is reactive not preventive.

proof_of_signal.pattern: scaledAmount computed from amount parameter; safeTransferFrom on line 73; no balance-before/after check

preconditions: Market uses a fee-on-transfer token as underlying asset
Token was normal at deployment but later upgraded to add fees, or was misconfigured

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Amount credited before transfer; no received-amount verification
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: scaledAmount = scaleAmount(amount) but actual received = amount - fee
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No pre/post balance check; no fee-on-transfer detection

analysis_questions: Is there any on-chain mechanism preventing fee-on-transfer tokens from being used?
Could a token upgrade introduce fees after market deployment?
What is the magnitude of accounting inflation per deposit?
validation_requirements: Deploy market with fee-on-transfer token and verify accounting mismatch
Calculate rate of insolvency accumulation

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A market is deployed with a token that later adds transfer fees via upgrade. Each deposit inflates the market's internal accounting. Eventually, the last withdrawers find insufficient assets. An attacker could deposit and immediately withdraw (possibly across batches) to extract more value than deposited.
prerequisites: Fee-on-transfer token used as underlying asset

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Market insolvency; last lenders lose funds proportional to accumulated fee gap

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Balance discrepancy between totalAssets and accounting

threats.title: nukeFromOrbit Double _writeState Call Causes Redundant Gas Consumption and Potential State Inconsistency
threats.hypothesis_description: When nukeFromOrbit() calls _blockAccount(), which calls _queueWithdrawal(), which calls _writeState(state), the state is written to storage. Then nukeFromOrbit() calls _writeState(state) again with the same memory reference. Both calls recalculate `isDelinquent` from `totalAssets()`. The second call is entirely redundant since nothing has changed between the two writes. This doubles the gas cost of the storage write operations (~40K+ gas for slot writes) for no benefit.
threats.investigation_rationale: The double write pattern is a code structure issue where _queueWithdrawal was designed to be a complete operation (writes state), but _blockAccount/nukeFromOrbit also need to write state. The fix would be to have _queueWithdrawal not write state when called from _blockAccount, or to remove the second write in nukeFromOrbit.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _blockAccount(state, accountAddress) calls _queueWithdrawal which calls _writeState; then nukeFromOrbit calls _writeState again on line 88

preconditions: Account is sanctioned and has non-zero balance
nukeFromOrbit is called

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Sequential _writeState calls with identical state object
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Redundant storage writes waste gas; second write overwrites identical values
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No flag to skip the second write

analysis_questions: What is the exact gas overhead of the redundant _writeState?
Could the state memory object be modified between the two writes by any code path?
Does the double write cause any emission of duplicate events?
validation_requirements: Measure gas usage of nukeFromOrbit with and without the redundant write
Verify state is identical between both writes

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Not directly exploitable, but the redundant gas cost makes nukeFromOrbit more expensive than necessary. In extreme cases with many sanctioned accounts to process, the extra gas could contribute to block gas limit issues.
prerequisites: Sanctioned account with balance

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Wasted gas costs; no fund loss

threats.title: Withdrawal execution can revert via division-by-zero when `batch.scaledTotalAmount == 0`, potentially blocking withdrawals for a given expiry
threats.hypothesis_description: In `_executeWithdrawal`, pro-rata computation divides by `batch.scaledTotalAmount` without an explicit existence/zero check. If an attacker (or any caller) supplies an `expiry` that has no batch (default struct) or a corrupted batch with `scaledTotalAmount == 0`, the `mulDiv(..., batch.scaledTotalAmount)` will revert, potentially blocking withdraw execution for that expiry and causing `executeWithdrawals` to revert mid-loop.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function-level comment says it should revert if `expiry` does not correspond to an existing withdrawal batch, but the implementation appears to rely on implicit division-by-zero revert rather than an explicit error. This may be exploitable as a griefing vector (especially against `executeWithdrawals` batch processing) and indicates missing explicit batch-existence validation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: MathUtils.mulDiv(batch.normalizedAmountPaid, status.scaledAmount, batch.scaledTotalAmount) with no explicit check that batch.scaledTotalAmount > 0 / batch exists

preconditions: Caller can call executeWithdrawal/executeWithdrawals with arbitrary `expiry`
`_withdrawalData.batches[expiry]` is the default (never created) OR has `scaledTotalAmount == 0` while code path still reached
No earlier revert prevents reaching the mulDiv line (e.g., market closed allowing pre-expiry execution)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: division by batch.scaledTotalAmount without explicit validation
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: revert can be triggered by choosing an expiry that has no batch; executeWithdrawals can be griefed by including such expiry

analysis_questions: Is it possible for a user to have `status.scaledAmount > 0` for an expiry where `batch.scaledTotalAmount == 0` (state corruption)?
Does the protocol rely on implicit division-by-zero revert as the intended "batch not found" check, and does it create an unintended DoS/griefing vector for executeWithdrawals loops?
Are there any code paths that create a batch but leave `scaledTotalAmount` at 0 (e.g., overflow/underflow/partial writes)?
validation_requirements: Unit test: call executeWithdrawal(account, randomExpiry) on closed market and confirm revert reason; evaluate whether this blocks legitimate batch operations
Fuzz: generate sequences of queueWithdrawal/closeMarket/executeWithdrawals with random expiries and look for unexpected permanent reverts

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker calls executeWithdrawals with arrays containing at least one invalid expiry so the whole call reverts, preventing third-party batch withdrawal executors (keepers) from processing a list efficiently.
prerequisites: Presence of third-party withdrawal execution service that uses executeWithdrawals batching
Market is closed or expiry is in the past (so the expiry check doesn't block earlier)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Indirect (DoS/griefing) but can delay withdrawals and increase operational costs
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any users relying on batch execution/keepers for withdrawals
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: High (use executeWithdrawal individually / filter expiries), but can still be used as repeated griefing

threats.test_coverage_gap: Likely missing negative tests for invalid expiry handling and executeWithdrawals partial-failure behavior

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: On-chain reverts observable; check revert selectors and whether it matches intended custom error behavior

threats.title: Batch query pagination functions can revert on out-of-range `start/end`, enabling read-path DoS for integrators
threats.hypothesis_description: Several pagination helpers compute `count = end - start` after capping `end = min(end, len)` but do not validate `start <= end`. If `start > end`, subtraction underflows and reverts. While these are view functions, they are often called by off-chain indexers/relayers; unexpected reverts can break integrations and operational tooling (e.g., iterating hooks templates/markets/borrowers).
threats.investigation_rationale: The pattern appears repeatedly across HooksFactory and WildcatArchController. It is a correctness defect (lack of bounds checks) and can cause widespread integration instability, even if not directly exploitable for fund theft.

proof_of_signal.pattern: end = min(end, len); uint256 count = end - start; (no require(start <= end))

preconditions: Caller supplies `start` greater than the capped `end` (e.g., start > len)
Function called directly by integrator or within off-chain tooling that doesn't sanitize inputs

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: WildcatArchController.sol pagination getters; HooksFactory.sol pagination getters
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: unchecked subtraction for array slice length
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: revert on malformed inputs instead of returning empty array

analysis_questions: Are these functions used by any on-chain state-changing paths (should not be)?
Do official SDKs/frontends rely on these getters with user-provided pagination, making them easy to trigger?
Should behavior be standardized (empty array) to prevent integration breakage?

threats.title: onSetAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips Hook Always Reverts Due to Pre-Call RETURNDATASIZE Check
threats.hypothesis_description: In HooksConfig.sol, the assembly for onSetAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips evaluates `lt(returndatasize(), 0x40)` BEFORE the `call()` to the hooks contract executes. In Yul, the `or(A, B)` evaluates left argument A first. Since RETURNDATASIZE at that point reflects the prior `balanceOf()` call (32 bytes) from `_getUpdatedState()`, `lt(32, 64)` is always true. The `or(true, ...)` is always true, causing an unconditional revert regardless of whether the hook call succeeds.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a code defect in assembly evaluation ordering. Any market that enables the useOnSetAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips hook flag will be permanently unable to change annualInterestBips or reserveRatioBips. The borrower loses the ability to manage market parameters, potentially trapping lenders at unfavorable rates or preventing the borrower from adjusting to market conditions.

proof_of_signal.lines: The assembly block containing `if or(lt(returndatasize(), 0x40), iszero(call(gas(), target, ...)))`
proof_of_signal.pattern: Yul evaluates or(A, B) left-to-right: A=lt(returndatasize(),0x40) is evaluated BEFORE B=iszero(call(...)). RETURNDATASIZE reflects the previous balanceOf (32 bytes), making lt(32,64)=true, so or(true,X)=true always.

preconditions: Market deployed with useOnSetAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips flag enabled in HooksConfig
Borrower attempts to call setAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: or(lt(returndatasize(), 0x40), iszero(call(...))) where RETURNDATASIZE is from a prior call, not the hook call
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: No manipulation needed; the bug triggers on every call to setAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips when the hook flag is enabled
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit extraction, but permanent DoS on market parameter changes creates economic lock-in for lenders and inability for borrower to respond to market conditions

Confirm setAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips always reverts

threats.title: Unpaid Withdrawal Batch Array Growth Enables Gas-Limit DoS on closeMarket
threats.hypothesis_description: closeMarket() contains an unbounded loop processing all unpaid batches: `for (uint256 i; i < numBatches; i++)`. A malicious lender can create many unpaid withdrawal batches by repeatedly queuing tiny withdrawals that become new batches (each requiring the pending batch to expire first). If withdrawalBatchDuration is short (or 0), a lender can create hundreds or thousands of distinct expired unpaid batches, causing closeMarket to exceed the block gas limit.
threats.investigation_rationale: Each unpaid batch requires an SLOAD to read from the queue, an SLOAD for batch data, multiple arithmetic operations, and an SSTORE to update. With withdrawalBatchDuration = 0, a lender could create a new batch per block. Over weeks/months, this accumulates enough batches to make the closeMarket loop exceed 30M gas.

Attacker is an authorized lender in the market

proof_of_signal.pattern: Unbounded loop over _withdrawalData.unpaidBatches with no gas-limit protection; array can grow unboundedly

Attacker is an authorized lender
Attacker has sufficient time to create many small withdrawal batches

threats.title: setAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips Reserve Ratio Check Uses Stale State for annualInterestBips During Decrease Validation
threats.hypothesis_description: In setAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips, when reserveRatioBips is decreased, the code checks `state.liquidityRequired() > totalAssets()` using the OLD reserveRatioBips and OLD annualInterestBips. Both `_annualInterestBips` and `_reserveRatioBips` are set simultaneously after this check. The hooks contract can return arbitrary values (within uint16 range), potentially setting annualInterestBips much higher than requested. Since `liquidityRequired()` does not depend on annualInterestBips, this specific check is sound. However, the hook could return a very different reserveRatioBips than the borrower intended.
threats.investigation_rationale: The hooks' `onSetAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips` returns `(newAnnualInterestBips, newReserveRatioBips)` which completely override the borrower's requested values. A malicious hook could return any uint16 values, limited only by the `> BIP` checks. If the hook is compromised or buggy, it could set parameters that trap the borrower or lenders in unfavorable conditions.

threats.title: createEscrow Publicly Callable - Anyone Can Deploy Escrows and Set Sanction Overrides
threats.hypothesis_description: WildcatSanctionsSentinel.createEscrow() is publicly callable with arbitrary (borrower, account, asset) parameters. It deploys an escrow and sets `sanctionOverrides[borrower][escrowContract] = true`. While the escrow address is deterministic and won't hold funds unless directed by a market, calling createEscrow with crafted parameters creates sanction overrides that the specified borrower didn't authorize. These overrides persist and could interfere with the borrower's sanction management.
threats.investigation_rationale: The escrow creation sets `sanctionOverrides[borrower][escrowContract] = true` using the caller-supplied `borrower` parameter, not `msg.sender`. An attacker can create overrides in any borrower's sanction override mapping. While the override is for the escrow address (not the attacker), this pollutes the borrower's sanction state. The escrow itself is safe (funds only go there through executeWithdrawal), but the override side-effect may be undesirable.

threats.title: Permanent Lock of Withdrawal Dust via Pro-Rata Rounding
threats.hypothesis_description: In `_executeWithdrawal`, the calculation of a user's share of a paid batch uses `MathUtils.mulDiv`, which rounds down. If a batch is paid but the pro-rata division leaves fractional wei remainders across multiple users, this dust is permanently left in `normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals`. Because `borrowableAssets` subtracts `normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals`, this dust is permanently locked in the market and can never be claimed by lenders or borrowed by the borrower.
threats.investigation_rationale: While the financial impact per transaction is small (dust), over thousands of withdrawal cycles, this permanently locks underlying assets in the contract, slowly degrading capital efficiency and creating unrecoverable protocol TVL.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint128 newTotalWithdrawn = uint128(MathUtils.mulDiv(...)) rounds down, leaving dust in state.normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit, but causes permanent capital destruction/lockup.

threats.title: Gas Griefing via Unpaid Batch Processing Loop
threats.hypothesis_description: In `repayAndProcessUnpaidWithdrawalBatches`, the `while` loop continues as long as `availableLiquidity > 0` and `i < numBatches`. If `availableLiquidity` is reduced to a dust amount too small to convert to a non-zero `scaledAmountBurned` (due to `scaleAmount` rounding), `_processUnpaidWithdrawalBatch` will return 0. The batch is not shifted from the queue, `availableLiquidity` remains unchanged, and the loop spins uselessly for the remaining `numBatches - i` iterations, wasting the caller's gas.
threats.investigation_rationale: This logic flaw causes the contract to repeatedly process the exact same unpaid batch without making any state changes, leading to unnecessary gas consumption and potential out-of-gas reverts for borrowers attempting to clear large queues.

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker griefs the borrower by forcing them to pay excessive gas fees to process withdrawals.

threats.title: Deposit Transfer Event Emits Input Amount Instead of Actual Minted Normalized Value
threats.hypothesis_description: In _depositUpTo, the ERC-20 Transfer event is emitted as `emit_Transfer(address(0), msg.sender, amount)` where `amount` is the user-supplied deposit amount (capped by maximumDeposit). However, the actual balance change is `normalizeAmount(scaledAmount)` = `normalizeAmount(scaleAmount(amount))` which can differ from `amount` due to rounding in the scale/normalize round-trip. This means the Transfer event reports a different value than the actual balance change, violating ERC-20 semantics. External integrations (block explorers, portfolio trackers, DEX aggregators) that rely on Transfer events for accounting will have discrepancies.
threats.investigation_rationale: ERC-20 Transfer events are universally used for off-chain tracking. Incorrect event amounts cause accounting discrepancies in indexers, wallets, and DeFi protocols that track token balances via events rather than direct queries.

threats.title: createEscrow Unrestricted Caller Can Grief tmpEscrowParams in Concurrent Transaction Contexts
threats.hypothesis_description: WildcatSanctionsSentinel.createEscrow is public with no access control or reentrancy guard. It modifies tmpEscrowParams (storage slot) before deploying the escrow contract, then resets it. If a market's executeWithdrawal calls _createEscrowForUnderlyingAsset → sentinel.createEscrow in the middle of a transaction, and the asset token has a callback (e.g., ERC-777 or similar), the callback could call createEscrow with different parameters, corrupting tmpEscrowParams. The WildcatSanctionsEscrow constructor reads tmpEscrowParams to set its immutable borrower/account/asset, potentially reading attacker-supplied values. However, the CREATE2 salt is derived from the CORRECT parameters, so the deployment address is still deterministic per the correct triple — a deployment with wrong params would go to a different address, and the correct-address deployment would fail.
threats.investigation_rationale: While the CREATE2 salt mitigates the worst case (wrong escrow params at the correct address), the lack of access control on createEscrow combined with mutable storage params is a risky pattern. On chains where callback tokens exist, this warrants verification that the mitigation (address mismatch) holds in all edge cases.
deployment_assumptions: Underlying asset token has transfer callbacks (ERC-777 or similar)
Multiple escrow creations possible within one transaction

threats.title: executeWithdrawals batch call can be griefed by a single failing withdrawal entry (all-or-nothing aggregation DoS)
threats.hypothesis_description: executeWithdrawals iterates and calls _executeWithdrawal for each (account, expiry). Any single entry that reverts (e.g., not expired, null withdrawal amount, invalid batch, sanctioned escrow creation failure, hook revert) causes the entire batch to revert, preventing processing of other valid withdrawals in the same transaction. Attackers can exploit this operational fragility by ensuring one entry is invalid when third-party executors attempt bulk processing.
threats.investigation_rationale: Batch processing is often used for operational efficiency (keepers executing many withdrawals). An all-or-nothing loop without per-item try/catch or skipping can be a practical DoS lever. Since the chain has no public mempool, this is less about frontrunning and more about persistent invalid entries and operational tooling assumptions.

threats.title: TransientBytesArray storage encoding/decoding mismatch or panic path can brick HooksFactory.getMarketParameters during market deployment flows
threats.hypothesis_description: HooksFactory relies on TransientBytesArray to store TmpMarketParameterStorage, then decode it in getMarketParameters() which is called by the market constructor via staticcall to the factory (caller()). If the transient bytes encoding becomes invalid (due to library bug, unexpected length, or reentrancy/overlapping usage), readToPointer can trigger a Panic_InvalidStorageByteArray revert, bricking market deployment mid-transaction.
threats.investigation_rationale: Market deployment is a critical operational path for borrowers. Transient storage byte-array encoding is implemented in assembly with custom invariants and an explicit panic branch. Subtle encoding bugs or edge cases (length thresholds around 31/32 bytes; incomplete clearing; nested usage) can create real-world deployment failures. While not a direct fund theft, it can halt protocol growth and borrower onboarding.
deployment_assumptions: Borrowers/ops deploy markets via HooksFactory on chains supporting Cancun transient storage semantics
No other code path in same transaction corrupts the transient slot key used for _tmpMarketParameters
threats.config_evidence: foundry.toml targets evm_version=cancun and solc=0.8.25; no live deployment evidence of chain/cancun support provided here.

proof_of_signal.pattern: extractByteArrayLength checks out-of-place encoding vs length<32; triggers Panic_InvalidStorageByteArray on mismatch

preconditions: HooksFactory has written tmp market parameters to the transient slot
Transient slot content is malformed for the decoder (length/flag mismatch) OR corrupted by nested writes
Market constructor calls factory.getMarketParameters() and expects 0x260 bytes

threats.title: HooksFactory._deployHooksInstance uses extcodesize-1 / extcodecopy starting at offset 1: malformed template code can cause unexpected initcode and unsafe deployments
threats.hypothesis_description: HooksFactory deploys hooks instances by copying the hooks template runtime code minus the first byte (extcodesize(template)-1 and extcodecopy(template, ..., 1, size)). This assumes templates are structured with a 1-byte prefix and that the remainder is valid initcode when appended with constructor arguments. A malicious or incorrectly formatted template can result in unexpected initcode, potentially deploying a contract with unintended logic or causing deployment failures that disrupt operations for registered borrowers.
threats.investigation_rationale: Template system is central to operational safety. Even with onlyArchControllerOwner gating template registration, mistakes or edge-case template bytecode formats can lead to widespread deployment issues. Additionally, if a template can be registered that yields a hooks instance with surprising behavior, markets can be deployed with hooks that do not enforce expected constraints.
deployment_assumptions: At least one hooks template is registered whose bytecode format does not match the assumed 'skip first byte' convention, or can be crafted to produce unintended initcode
Registered borrowers use that template to deploy hooks instances
threats.config_evidence: Template registration is controlled by onlyArchControllerOwner, but no evidence here about template bytecode conventions or audits of templates.

proof_of_signal.pattern: initCodeSize := sub(extcodesize(hooksTemplate), 1); extcodecopy(hooksTemplate, initCodePointer, 1, initCodeSize); create(0, initCodePointer, initCodeSizeWithArgs)

preconditions: A hooksTemplate is registered and enabled

threats.title: executeWithdrawals Strips Hook ExtraData, Bypassing Hook Access Control
threats.hypothesis_description: The batch executeWithdrawals function passes msg.data.length as baseCalldataSize to _executeWithdrawal, which is then forwarded to hooks.onExecuteWithdrawal. Since calldatasize() == msg.data.length, extraCalldataBytes is always 0. In contrast, the single executeWithdrawal uses a fixed 0x44, allowing appended calldata to pass through as extraData. If a hooks contract uses extraData for authorization (credential proofs, signatures, access tokens), the batch version completely bypasses this check by always passing empty extraData.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete code-level inconsistency between two public entry points that perform the same operation. If hooks rely on extraData for access control (which the protocol explicitly supports via the extraData pattern across all hooks), the batch version silently strips this data, creating an authorization bypass.

proof_of_signal.pattern: executeWithdrawals passes msg.data.length as baseCalldataSize causing extraCalldataBytes=0 in hook call, vs executeWithdrawal which passes 0x44 allowing extraData passthrough

preconditions: Market deployed with useOnExecuteWithdrawal hook enabled

threats.title: normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals Over-Reservation via scaleAmount Rounding in Batch Payments
threats.hypothesis_description: In _getUpdatedState (called on every state-modifying function), _applyWithdrawalBatchPayment is invoked for pending batches. The function uses scaleAmount(availableLiquidity) which calls rayDiv (rounds to nearest), potentially rounding UP the scaledAvailableLiquidity. Then normalizedAmountPaid = mulDiv(scaledAmountBurned, scaleFactor, RAY) rounds DOWN. When scaleFactor >= 2*RAY, normalizedAmountPaid can exceed the input availableLiquidity. Since normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals += normalizedAmountPaid, the market progressively over-reserves for withdrawals, creating a gap between accounting and actual tokens held.
threats.investigation_rationale: With scaleFactor=2e27 and availableLiquidity=1: scaleAmount(1)=1, normalizedAmountPaid=floor(1*2e27/1e27)=2 > 1. Every partial batch payment accumulates this error. Over time, normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals exceeds actual reserved tokens, causing withdrawableProtocolFees to underflow (panic) and borrowableAssets to return 0.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _getUpdatedState calls _applyWithdrawalBatchPayment on every state-modifying call; scaleAmount rounds up while mulDiv rounds down, causing normalizedAmountPaid > availableLiquidity when scaleFactor >= 2*RAY

preconditions: Market has been running long enough for scaleFactor to exceed 2*RAY (100%+ accumulated interest)
Partial batch payments occur frequently (pending withdrawal batches with insufficient liquidity)
No explicit cap on normalizedAmountPaid vs available liquidity in _applyWithdrawalBatchPayment

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Accumulated over-reservation eventually causes withdrawableProtocolFees underflow panic and protocol insolvency

threats.title: transferFrom Allowance Consumed by Requested Amount, Not Actual Scaled Transfer Value
threats.hypothesis_description: In transferFrom, the allowance is reduced by the user-specified `amount` (normalized), but the actual value transferred is `normalizeAmount(scaleAmount(amount))` which may differ due to rounding in rayDiv. If scaleAmount rounds UP, the actual scaled transfer represents MORE value than `amount`, meaning the spender transfers more value than their allowance was reduced by. Over many small transfers, a spender could systematically extract slightly more value than their allowance permits.
threats.investigation_rationale: The allowance reduction and actual transfer use different rounding paths. The allowance decreases by `amount`, but the actual transfer uses `scaleAmount(amount)` which may round up, giving the receiver `normalizeAmount(scaleAmount(amount))` > `amount` in value. This is a concrete accounting asymmetry between allowance consumption and value transfer.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Allowance reduced by `amount` parameter but _transfer uses scaleAmount(amount) which rounds to nearest, creating potential mismatch

threats.title: queueWithdrawal Passes Raw User Amount to Hooks Instead of Normalized Scaled Value
threats.hypothesis_description: In queueWithdrawal, the normalized amount passed to _queueWithdrawal (and subsequently to hooks and events) is the raw user-specified `amount`, not `state.normalizeAmount(scaledAmount)`. This inconsistency means hooks receive inaccurate data when processing queueWithdrawal calls, potentially causing incorrect access control decisions or accounting in the hooks contract.

If hooks use this value for validation (e.g., minimum withdrawal amounts, rate limiting), the inaccuracy could allow bypassing constraints or create accounting discrepancies in the hooks' internal state.

threats.title: writeState Delinquency Check Creates Stale isDelinquent When Multiple Asset Transfers Occur
threats.hypothesis_description: In _writeState, `isDelinquent = state.liquidityRequired() > totalAssets()`. The state memory object used for liquidityRequired() may not reflect changes from external token transfers (donations or direct transfers to the market). If tokens are sent directly to the market (bypassing deposit/repay functions), the isDelinquent flag could be incorrect - showing delinquent when assets are sufficient or vice versa. This affects timeDelinquent tracking and penalty APR activation, potentially causing incorrect fee calculations.
threats.investigation_rationale: Direct token transfers to the market increase totalAssets() without updating any internal accounting. If a market is borderline delinquent, a strategic donation could flip isDelinquent=false in _writeState without the state reflecting the donated amount in any controlled accounting field. Conversely, an ERC20 token balance decrease (e.g., through a rebasing token) could make the market appear delinquent.
deployment_assumptions: Underlying asset is standard ERC20 (no rebasing, no fee-on-transfer)
threats.config_evidence: WildcatMarketBase.sol totalAssets() directly reads balanceOf which includes unaccounted transfers. Protocol documentation states no fee-on-transfer/rebasing tokens.

threats.title: Massive Fake Transfer Event Spoofing via Ray Math Overflow
threats.hypothesis_description: An attacker can pass a massive amount to transfer() or queueWithdrawal() that silently overflows during scaling, resulting in a tiny actual deduction but emitting an event with the massive original amount.
threats.investigation_rationale: The rayDiv function in MathUtils uses unchecked assembly `mul(a, RAY)` which silently wraps around modulo 2^256. If a user passes an amount like ~1.15e50, `amount * RAY` overflows and results in a tiny scaledAmount (e.g., 1 wei). The transfer succeeds, deducting only 1 wei from the user, but emits a Transfer event with the massive original amount.

proof_of_signal.pattern: c := div(add(mul(a, RAY), halfB), b) executes in unchecked assembly, allowing mul(a, RAY) to wrap

preconditions: Attacker has at least 1 wei of scaled balance in the market

threats.title: Anyone can hard-freeze a market by forcing a revert in nukeFromOrbit() via internal _getAccount() sanctions check
threats.hypothesis_description: nukeFromOrbit(accountAddress) is externally callable by anyone, but its internal path calls _blockAccount() -> _queueWithdrawal() which writes the target account back to storage. _queueWithdrawal() decrements the Account and then assigns it to storage, but obtaining the Account typically uses _getAccount(), which reverts if the account is sanctioned. The override _blockAccount() in WildcatMarket retrieves Account directly from storage (no sanctions check) and then calls _queueWithdrawal(), which stores the Account without re-checking sanctions. However, within _queueWithdrawal() hooks.onQueueWithdrawal is invoked and may rely on _getAccount semantics in hook implementations. More critically, if any other internal path in _queueWithdrawal() calls _getAccount (or a future hook change does), nukeFromOrbit can become permanently uncallable for sanctioned addresses, leaving sanctioned funds unescrowable and blocking compliance workflows. This needs validation against actual hook templates and any inherited code not shown here.
threats.investigation_rationale: nukeFromOrbit is designed as a public compliance tool; if it can be made to revert systematically for sanctioned accounts, operators lose the ability to quarantine sanctioned balances during incidents. Because it is permissionless, an attacker can repeatedly attempt calls to create noise and operational DoS if it consistently reverts for sanctioned accounts.
deployment_assumptions: The deployed hooks implementation (hooks.hooksAddress()) either (a) calls back into the market in a way that triggers _getAccount(accountAddress) for the sanctioned address during onNukeFromOrbit/onQueueWithdrawal, or (b) otherwise reverts for sanctioned lenders.
threats.config_evidence: No specific hooks template code provided in scope; market behavior depends on external hooks contract selected at deployment (HooksConfig.hooksAddress).

proof_of_signal.pattern: nukeFromOrbit() is external and permissionless; calls hooks.onNukeFromOrbit then _blockAccount(state, accountAddress)

preconditions: Target accountAddress is sanctioned per sentinel.isSanctioned(borrower, accountAddress)
Hooks are enabled for onNukeFromOrbit and/or onQueueWithdrawal and they revert for sanctioned accounts or trigger market codepaths that revert on sanctioned accounts
Operators rely on nukeFromOrbit for quarantine rather than passive blocking only

threats.title: Permissionless updateState() can be used to amplify hook-induced reverts and stall market progress
threats.hypothesis_description: updateState() is permissionless and triggers _getUpdatedState() which may process expired withdrawal batches and invoke hooks indirectly through state transitions elsewhere. If the configured hooks contract is brittle (reverts based on state, timestamp, calldata extraData length assumptions, or external dependencies), any caller can repeatedly attempt updateState() causing revert storms, preventing state progression via the cheapest external entrypoint. Even if other functions can still progress state, integrators and keepers may rely on updateState() as a safe state-sync call, so a revert-prone hook or dependency can create operational instability.
threats.investigation_rationale: Many protocols use permissionless 'poke' functions. If hooks introduce fragility, attackers can cheaply trigger repeated failures, degrade monitoring/automation, and cause cascading operational failures (e.g., inability to process expired batches until another function is successfully called).
deployment_assumptions: Hooks configuration enables at least one hook used during typical state update flows and the hook can revert under attacker-controllable conditions (e.g., calldata size/extraData, gas, external dependency).
threats.config_evidence: Hooks are externally supplied per-market via immutable HooksConfig; hook code not included here, so revert conditions depend on deployment choice.

proof_of_signal.pattern: updateState() external nonReentrant; calls _getUpdatedState() then _writeState(state) with no auth gating

preconditions: A hook or an external dependency used by hooks can revert based on current state or caller-provided calldata length
Market operations rely on successful state updates to process expired batches/fees
Attacker has funds for repeated transaction spam

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Permissionless state-poke combined with hook external calls increases revert surface
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Hook selection at deployment determines fragility

threats.title: Origination fee payment in deployMarket can be abused with malicious ERC20 to lock borrower deployments (reverting transfers)
threats.hypothesis_description: deployMarket/_deployMarket optionally collects origination fees by calling `originationFeeAsset.safeTransferFrom(msg.sender, templateDetails.feeRecipient, originationFeeAmount)`. If originationFeeAsset is a malicious or non-standard token (reverting on transferFrom, requiring allowlists, or charging prohibitive fees), borrower market deployments can be blocked. threats.title: TransientBytesArray decoding can revert (panic) if transient storage is corrupted/uninitialized, potentially bricking market deployment via factory
threats.hypothesis_description: HooksFactory relies on transient storage `_tmpMarketParameters` being correctly encoded prior to calling `LibStoredInitCode.create2WithStoredInitCode(...)` so the market constructor can staticcall `factory.getMarketParameters()` and decode the transient bytes. `LibTransientBytesArray.readToPointer` includes strict validity checks that can panic-revert on invalid storage byte-array encoding. If any path leaves transient storage non-empty/invalid or writes malformed bytes, all market deployments in that transaction will revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: Deployment is a critical protocol operation. The transient-storage encoder/decoder is custom assembly and contains explicit panic conditions; any bug in write/read pairing, slot collisions, or unexpected reentrancy into factory deployment code could corrupt transient storage and make deployments unreliable.
deployment_assumptions: Transient storage semantics (EIP-1153) are supported and behave as expected on the target chain
No unexpected re-entrancy into HooksFactory deployment flow that overwrites `_tmpMarketParameters` before the market constructor reads it
No other code uses the same transient slot key `keccak256('Transient:TmpMarketParametersStorage') - 1` in the same transaction context
threats.config_evidence: HooksFactory uses a constant transient slot derived from keccak and writes/clears it around create2 deployment; no cross-contract coordination guarantees are shown beyond nonReentrant on deploy functions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: extractByteArrayLength() panic-reverts when transient slot encoding does not match expected (outOfPlace vs length < 32 invariant)

preconditions: A call path results in `_tmpMarketParameters` containing invalid encoding at time of getMarketParameters() call

No alternative parameter source exists (constructor hard-depends on factory response)

threats.title: HooksFactory deployHooksInstance uses `extcodesize(hooksTemplate)-1` and copies code starting at byte 1, risking malformed initcode deployment for templates not matching assumed format
threats.hypothesis_description: HooksFactory’s `_deployHooksInstance` constructs initcode by copying `extcodecopy(hooksTemplate, ..., 1, extcodesize-1)` and then appending constructor args. This assumes a specific "stored init code" format with a 1-byte STOP prefix (or similar). If a template is not in that exact format, the deployed instance bytecode may be invalid, unexpectedly different, or could omit critical initialization logic, leading to hook misbehavior that affects deposits/withdrawals/transfers.
threats.investigation_rationale: Hook instances are trusted gatekeepers for access control and parameter constraints. A subtle initcode-format mismatch can silently deploy hooks instances with unexpected semantics. Since templates are registered by arch-controller owner, this is more of a governance/configuration risk, but it is still a concrete code hazard due to brittle assembly assumptions.
deployment_assumptions: A hooksTemplate is registered whose deployed runtime code is not the expected "initcode storage" format with leading byte reserved
The protocol relies on correct hook semantics for access control and safety constraints
threats.config_evidence: HooksFactory.sol `_deployHooksInstance` uses `initCodeSize := sub(extcodesize(hooksTemplate), 1)` and `extcodecopy(..., 1, initCodeSize)` without validating the template format.

threats.title: Hooks onCreateMarket Can Disable Optional Hooks During Market Deployment
threats.hypothesis_description: In _deployMarket, the hooks instance's onCreateMarket is called and its return value replaces the HooksConfig for the market being deployed. The mergeFlags function uses `(configFlags & optionalFlags) | requiredFlags`, meaning only 'required' hooks are guaranteed to be active. A malicious borrower who deploys a hooks instance (via a template that permits it) could configure the instance to return a HooksConfig with disabled optional hooks during onCreateMarket, removing protections like onDeposit access control or onTransfer sanctions checks.
threats.investigation_rationale: The borrower controls the hooks instance's constructor args and the onCreateMarket behavior (within template constraints). If the template classifies critical hooks (like deposit access control) as 'optional' rather than 'required', the borrower could disable them, creating an apparently-legitimate market that lacks essential protections.

threats.title: Transient storage parameter passing may fail on chains/EVM configs without stable tstore/tload semantics, bricking market deployments
threats.hypothesis_description: HooksFactory uses TransientBytesArray (tstore/tload) to pass TmpMarketParameterStorage to the WildcatMarket constructor via factory.getMarketParameters(). This assumes Cancun/EIP-1153 transient storage is supported and behaves consistently. On chains that lag Cancun support, are configured differently, or have non-standard execution environments, market deployment could fail or return incorrect parameters, leading to misinitialized immutables (asset/borrower/sentinel/etc.) and potentially irrecoverable deployment failures.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is an operational chain-compatibility risk. Wildcat targets multiple chains; any mismatch in EVM version support can cause systemic deployment failures or misconfiguration, impacting production operations broadly.
deployment_assumptions: Deployment occurs on a chain or L2 that does not support transient storage (EIP-1153) or has divergent semantics
Factory and market are compiled with evm_version=cancun assumptions (as in foundry.toml/hardhat.config.ts)
threats.config_evidence: foundry.toml sets evm_version='cancun' and solc=0.8.25; TransientBytesArray.sol uses tload/tstore directly without feature detection.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Direct use of tload/tstore for dynamic bytes encoding/decoding; no runtime feature gating

preconditions: Target chain does not fully support Cancun transient storage opcodes or has bugs in their implementation
HooksFactory is used as the canonical deployment path for markets (as designed)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Critical deployment wiring relies on transient storage opcodes; failure breaks all new market deployments
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Deployment tooling chooses compiler/evmVersion; chain choice matters

operational_questions: Which target chains (Base/Arbitrum/Polygon) fully support EIP-1153 at the intended deployment times?
Are there chain-specific audits/tests for tstore/tload behavior under reverts and nested calls?
Is there a fallback non-transient deployment path?
validation_requirements: Deploy and create markets on each target chain/testnet with identical bytecode
Chaos testing: force reverts mid-deploy and ensure tmp parameters cannot leak across calls
Verify market constructor reads correct 0x260 bytes from factory staticcall under all conditions

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Select a chain/environment with partial Cancun support or custom EVM behavior
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attempt deployMarket; observe misreads or deployment reverts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Operationally, this manifests as an inability to onboard borrowers/markets on that chain
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Not an attacker-driven exploit in normal conditions; environment-driven

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: High: market deployment pipeline bricked on affected networks
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High: requires redeploying factory with alternative parameter passing or changing chain strategy

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Deployment reverts in market constructor when calling factory.getMarketParameters() (returndatasize mismatch)
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Markets deployed with incorrect immutable params (asset/borrower/sentinel mismatch) — catastrophic if happens
